description: Let's actually nix the play and the skip back controls. Just tapping on the anywhere on an item on that screen should pause and play it. It should toggle the play pause. and then there should be, so we can get rid of the button, but then there should just be a brief thing that hovers over the screen and then hides again after a second or two. Kind of like TikTok and those apps do it. And then double tap should skip back 15 seconds.

results.identified_issue: Reporter wanted a TikTok-style gestural transport. The play/pause + skip-back buttons lived in NowPlayingContent's 'transport' HStack, used only by ArgumentFeedCard; QuestionIntroCard already had a single-tap toggle. I removed the transport entirely and moved the controls onto the whole page as a reusable View modifier (playbackTapControls) so both card types behave identically. Chose SwiftUI's stacked onTapGesture(count: 2) before onTapGesture() so the double tap disambiguates from the single (the ~0.3s single-tap delay is the standard cost and matches how TikTok-style double-tap apps feel). The flash captures isPlaying at tap time to show the state the toggle moves TO. Kept the read-only PlaybackIndicator bar untouched (reporter only asked to nix the buttons). The isPreparing spinner that used to sit inside the play button is now a small 'Warming up the voice' note so a tap that can't play yet doesn't read as broken. I could NOT run the app to see it live — this repo's flow forbids building (vigil builds/deploys the pushed commit), so the gesture disambiguation and flash timing are verified by reading, not by driving the UI.
results.summary: Removed the play and skip-back buttons — tap the page to play/pause, double-tap to skip back 15s
results.detailed_description: Done. The play/pause and skip-back buttons are gone from the Discover feed. Now a single tap anywhere on a card toggles play/pause, and a double tap skips back 15 seconds — exactly the gestural transport you described. Each tap flashes a brief icon in the centre of the screen (play, pause, or the 15-second skip-back glyph) that scales in, holds for about a second, then fades out on its own, the way TikTok surfaces a control just long enough to see it and then gets out of the way. I applied this to both the argument cards and the question intro card so the whole feed feels the same. The read-only progress bar stays (you only asked to remove the buttons, not the position indicator), and while the neural voice is still synthesizing a card there's now a small 'Warming up the voice' note where the play button used to spin, so a tap that can't play anything yet doesn't feel broken. One heads-up: I built this by reading the code carefully rather than driving it, because this repo's workflow has me hand off for the build/deploy step rather than run the app myself — so please give the single-vs-double-tap feel a try on device (there's an intentional brief pause before a single tap registers, which is what lets the double tap be told apart), and let me know if you want the flash to linger longer or shorter.
